Part 11 is almost entirely concerned with the audiovisual recording of interviews between police officers and suspects, and of admissions made by suspects. It does not specifically require that interviews be recorded, but achieves that underlying policy objective in s 118(3) by making an unrecorded admission inadmissible unless there is a 'reasonable excuse', or the court decides otherwise under s 155. Part 11 allows interviews to be conducted by remote communication (s 116), and also regulates the distribution and use of recordings of interviews. Beyond that it makes no changes to the common law powers of police officers to conduct interviews. In that regard it is relevant to note that s 7 of the Act provides that a police officer has the powers, duties and responsibilities that a constable has under the common law. Section 8 also provides that the Act does not affect any power that an officer may lawfully exercise in common with any other citizen. Accordingly, police officers have the common law right to ask questions of suspects, and suspects have the common law right not to answer the same. Section 118 replaced s 570D of the Criminal Code, and like its predecessor has the underlying purposes of limiting the potential for police 'verballing', and reducing disputes about admissions generally (Kelly v The Queen (2004) 218 CLR 216 [42], Nicholls v The Queen (2005) 219 CLR 196 [98] - [99], [154], Carr v Western Australia (2007) 232 CLR 138 [57]). In Carr [131] Kirby J held that s 570D also had a wider purpose of 'ensuring that the repression of crime through the conviction of the guilty is done in a way which reflects our fundamental values as a society'. Although there are close similarities between s 118 and the former s 570D, there are also significant differences. Furthermore, some of the words in s 118 which are the same as before, now appear in a different statutory setting. Accordingly, authorities as to the meaning of s 570D may throw light on the meaning of s 118, but are not necessarily a reliable guide to the proper construction of the current provisions. The key operative provision is s 118(3) which makes unrecorded admissions prima facie inadmissible. However, the categories of admissions falling within the scope of s 118(3) are limited. Firstly, it applies only to admissions by a 'suspect' as defined in s 115, namely a person suspected of having committed an offence (which necessarily Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 41 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
refers to a suspicion held by a police officer). Secondly, and by reason of s 118(4), there must be reasonable grounds for that suspicion.

A third limitation on the operation of s 118(3) arises from the definition of 'admission' in s 118(1). Section 118(3) only applies to an admission made 'to' a police officer, and this raises an important issue of construction in the circumstances of the present case.

Quite obviously, when a police officer asks a question of a suspect and the suspect responds with an admission, that admission is made 'to' the police officer. However, there are a wide variety of other circumstances in which an admission can be made where the position is not so clear. For example, (as alleged in the present case) the admission might be made to a third person and overheard by the police officer. It may also be that the suspect making the admission is unaware that a police officer is present. A further possibility is that the admission is an involuntary exclamation or a musing out loud which is not directed towards any person at all. It should also be borne in mind that an admission need not be by spoken words but might be a physical act or other behaviour by the suspect. In light of the wide variety of possible circumstances, a question of construction arises as to what constitutes an admission made 'to' a police officer within the meaning of s 118.

The word 'to' is a preposition which varies in meaning depending upon the context in which it appears. In the context of s 118, I consider it to mean an admission made by a suspect which is simply heard or observed by a police officer. For the reasons which follow, I also consider it unnecessary that the admission be directed towards a police officer, or that the suspect intend that it be heard or seen by a police officer.

In this regard, the text of the Act and its legislative history point towards the purpose of encouraging the audiovisual recording of admissions, and of expanding (not restricting) the circumstances in which such recordings should occur (Carr [56]). A wide construction of the definition also promotes the underlying policy of minimising the opportunities for police 'verballing'.

It can be assumed that such opportunities will only arise once a person becomes a 'suspect' (which no doubt is one of the reasons for subs (4)). Therefore it would be surprising if Parliament did not intend s 118 to cover all admissions made by a suspect in the presence of a police officer after that point in time. The word 'to' is a preposition which varies in meaning depending upon the context in which it appears. In the context of s 118, I consider it to mean an admission made by a suspect which is simply heard or observed by a police officer. For the reasons which follow, I also consider it unnecessary that the admission be directed towards a police officer, or that the suspect intend that it be heard or seen by a police officer. In this regard, the text of the Act and its legislative history point towards the purpose of encouraging the audiovisual recording of admissions, and of expanding (not restricting) the circumstances in which such recordings should occur (Carr [56]). A wide construction of the definition also promotes the underlying policy of minimising the opportunities for police 'verballing'. It can be assumed that such opportunities will only arise once a person becomes a 'suspect' (which no doubt is one of the reasons for subs (4)). Therefore it would be surprising if Parliament did not intend s 118 to cover all admissions made by a suspect in the presence of a police officer after that point in time. A narrower construction limiting s 118 to Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 42 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
admissions made directly to a police officer would leave open considerable potential for abuse. All that would be necessary to avoid s 118 would be evidence that the admission was made to some third person or was not directed towards any person at all.

In my view, the wider construction is also consistent with that part of the definition which refers to a suspect's 'acts or otherwise'. It is difficult to conceive in what way a physical act amounting to an admission can be made 'to' a police officer in the narrower sense, and only a wide construction can comfortably accommodate the full text of the definition.

On the present appeal it is unnecessary to decide whether there can be an 'admission' (within the meaning of s 118) when the suspect is unaware that a police officer is present. However, I am inclined to the view (consistent with the reasons already expressed) that such admissions fall within the ambit of s 118.

In arriving at this wider construction I have not overlooked the word 'made' in the definition. Although this word (in combination with 'to') could be construed as requiring that the admission be directed towards a police officer, I consider that in the context of the provisions as a whole, it simply means the bringing into existence of the admission.

Once it is established that an unrecorded admission falls within the ambit of s 118, the next question that will usually arise is whether there is any reasonable excuse for the absence of a recording. The definition of 'reasonable excuse' in s 118(1) uses the word 'includes' to refer to four specific examples, all of which fall within with the ordinary meaning of those words.

The word 'includes' is commonly used as a drafting mechanism to enlarge the ordinary meaning of a word or phrase in a statute. For this reason, there is a line of authority (commencing with Dilworth v Commissioner of Stamps [1899] AC 99) to the effect that when 'includes' is used to define a statutory word or expression in a way that does not enlarge its ordinary meaning, it can be exhaustive. In Y Z Finance Co Pty Ltd v Cummings (1964) 109 CLR 395, Kitto J held:

Unlike the verb 'means', 'includes' has no exclusive force of its own. It indicates that the whole of its object is within its subject, but not that its object is the whole of its subject. Whether its object is the whole of its subject is a question of the true construction of the entire provision in which the word appears. The well-known statement of Lord Watson in Dilworth v Commissioner of Stamps [1899] AC 99 at pp 105, 106 should not be taken so literally as to reduce the inquiry in a case like the present to Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 43

[2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
an inquiry into the meaning of the word 'includes'. Strictly speaking, that word cannot be equivalent to 'means and includes'. But a provision in which it appears may or may not be enacted as a complete and therefore exclusive statement of what the subject expression includes. A provision which is of that character has the same effect as if 'means' had been the verb instead of 'includes'. The question whether a particular provision is exclusive although 'includes' is the only verb employed is therefore a question of the intention to be gathered from the provision as a whole (401 - 402).

Very often, the best means of determining the relevant statutory intent is to examine the pattern of drafting of all definitions in the Act as a whole. If the draftsperson has carefully discriminated between the use of 'means' and 'includes' in the various definitions, this favours the conclusion that the latter word is not intended to be exhaustive (Y Z Finance (403 - 404); The Queen v Gray; Ex parte Marsh (1985) 157 CLR 351, 365, Cohns Industries Pty Ltd v Deputy Federal Commissioner of Taxation (1979) 24 ALR 658, 660). It is also relevant to consider whether the full content of the ordinary meaning leaves scope for the defined words to have an application beyond the specific terms of the definition (The Queen v Gray (365), Buckle v Josephs (1983) 47

sections.h:
In the present instance, considerable assistance can be gained from the authority of Nicholls where it was held (at [3], [106], [156], [218] and [342]) that 'reasonable excuse' in s 570D(4) was inclusively defined, and that there could be circumstances not within the explicit definition which might amount to a reasonable excuse. The definition of 'reasonable excuse' in s 570D(4) was substantially the same as in s 118. In my opinion, there is nothing in the precise wording or respective statutory contexts of the two provisions which differentiates them from the point of view of whether they are exhaustive or inclusive in effect. Accordingly, I consider that Nicholls is good authority for the proposition that the definition of 'reasonable excuse' in s 118 is not exhaustive.

This conclusion is confirmed by an analysis of the pattern of definitions in the Act. Section 3 contains numerous definitions which are of general application. There are also numerous other definitions throughout the Act (in a total of 38 sections) which apply only to specific sections or parts. In almost every instance, the Act defines a word or phrase by use of the verb 'means'. Apart from the definition of 'reasonable excuse' in s 118, there are only six other instances where the draftsperson has used the word 'includes' (four instances in s 3, and one in each of s 57 and s 154). When those other instances are examined, it is very clear that Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 44

sections.h:
the draftsperson has made a careful distinction between terms which are to have an exhaustive definition, and those which are to be inclusively defined.

Furthermore the ordinary meaning of 'reasonable excuse' (in the context of the absence of a recording) is obviously capable of a wider application than the four specified examples in the definition. For all of these reasons I consider that the definition leaves open the possibility of circumstances not within those four examples which could give rise to a 'reasonable excuse'.

The first specified reasonable excuse in the definition is that it was not 'practicable' to make an audiovisual recording of the admission. The ordinary meaning of 'practicable' is 'capable of being put into practice, done, or effected, esp. with the available means or with reason or prudence; feasible' (The Macquarie Dictionary (2nd ed) 1388).

Consistent with this meaning, the issue whether a given task is 'practicable', involves an enquiry as to the means and resources available to accomplish it.

That Parliament had this concern is confirmed by the Second Reading Speech of the Minister when the legislation was being introduced. The Bill had failed to adopt the recommendation of a Royal Commission that the execution of all search warrants be video-taped, and the Minister's stated reason for this was that it was 'logistically impracticable'. During the Second Reading debate in the Legislative Council there was also the following statement by the Parliamentary Secretary responsible for the Bill:

To videotape all search warrants would require an extra officer to assist in all cases. In remote areas some police stations only have two officers.

Therefore, a search warrant would effectively have to be executed by one officer while the other officer operated the video camera. If we were to give effect to this provision should the house proceed down this path, we would need to ensure that all officers have access to a video camera and that would place significant additional financial cost on the police service because it would have to purchase additional recording equipment.

…

It may have the effect that only one search could be executed at any one time. Some larger police stations would require a significant number of video cameras to ensure that officers could access the equipment.

…

Some larger police stations would require a significant number of video cameras to ensure that officers could access the equipment. … Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 45 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
On the issue of practicability, the question of the duration of a search arises. Currently, when officers record the execution of search warrants, they record the entry and advice to the occupant that the video camera will be turned on when questions are asked and items located. If an officer were to continuously videotape, a lot of the tape would be used, because there may be delays while difficult areas are accessed by the officers.

(Hansard 6990)

Parliament nevertheless amended the Bill to include the present s 45(2) requiring that an audiovisual recording of the execution of a search warrant be made if 'reasonable practicable'. This was a compromise which the Parliamentary Secretary in the Legislative Council described as:

[A] set of words that is least operationally restrictive for the police and still encourages them, where possible, to use a video recording. (Hansard 7320) It can be assumed that Parliament had the same concerns about video recording of admissions as it did in respect of search warrants.

Accordingly, the question of whether or not it was 'practicable' to record an admission will always involve a consideration of what resources (in terms of personnel and equipment) were available.

Other relevant factors may be the time that was available to organise those resources, the degree of expectation that an admission might be made, and whether or not this justified the effort and cost involved in utilizing the resources.

This determination as to 'practicability' must necessarily be made in hindsight based upon the investigating officers' knowledge of the relevant circumstances prior to the admission being made. It may be that the admission was entirely foreseeable, or alternatively that it could not have been reasonably anticipated. In the end, the essential question is whether it would have been reasonable for the investigating officer to have had audio visual equipment and personnel on hand ready to record the admission, at the time, and in all of the circumstances in which it was in fact made. If the answer to that question is in the negative, then the admission was made when it was 'not practicable' to make an audiovisual recording of it.

sections.h:
nevertheless result in the discretionary exclusion of a confession on grounds of unfairness: (Driscoll v The Queen (1977) 137 CLR 517, 540).

Section 138(2)(c) of the Act has for the first time created a statutory right to a reasonable opportunity to communicate or to attempt to communicate with a legal practitioner. This right is conferred on an 'arrested suspect' within the meaning of s 138 which includes a person who has been arrested under s 128.

Section 137(3)(c) confers a similar right on any arrested person to a reasonable opportunity of communicating or attempting to communicate with a relative or friend. That right of communication is restricted to the purpose of informing the relative or friend of the arrested person's whereabouts, whereas the right to communicate with a legal practitioner is not explicitly restricted in any way.

The Act not only confers those two rights of communication on an arrested suspect, but in my view also provides the means by which they can be fulfilled. In this regard s 138(3) requires that the officer in charge of the investigation inform the suspect of each right of communication as 'soon as practicable' after arrest. In the great majority of cases nothing would prevent the suspect being so informed immediately after arrest and before there is any interview. Circumstances which might justify any delay in doing so could include intoxication, illness, mental problems or language difficulties of the suspect.

Apart from the obligation to inform an arrested suspect of the rights of communication, the officer in charge also must as soon as practicable afford the suspect his or her 'other' rights under s 137 and s 138(2). This reference in s 138(3)(b) to 'other' rights raises an obvious question of construction.

On a literal reading of s 138(3) the obligation on the officer in charge would be simply to inform the suspect of the rights of communication and not to afford the suspect those rights. However, in my view this literal construction cannot be correct because it would contradict the underlying legislative intent that arrested suspects be able to exercise the rights conferred by the Act. Accordingly, it is necessary to find an alternative construction, and in my opinion the key to a proper construction is to be found in what must be the underlying reason for the differentiation in the duties imposed on the officer in charge under s 138(3).

When the particular nature of each of the relevant rights is examined, it is clear that the officer in charge is required to 'afford' to the suspect Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 47

[2010] WASCA 199

The Act not only confers those two rights of communication on an arrested suspect, but in my view also provides the means by which they can be fulfilled. In this regard s 138(3) requires that the officer in charge of the investigation inform the suspect of each right of communication as 'soon as practicable' after arrest. Apart from the obligation to inform an arrested suspect of the rights of communication, the officer in charge also must as soon as practicable afford the suspect his or her 'other' rights under s 137 and s 138(2). This reference in s 138(3)(b) to 'other' rights raises an obvious question of construction. On a literal reading of s 138(3) the obligation on the officer in charge would be simply to inform the suspect of the rights of communication and not to afford the suspect those rights. However, in my view this literal construction cannot be correct because it would contradict the underlying legislative intent that arrested suspects be able to exercise the rights conferred by the Act. Accordingly, it is necessary to find an alternative construction, and in my opinion the key to a proper construction is to be found in what must be the underlying reason for the differentiation in the duties imposed on the officer in charge under s 138(3). When the particular nature of each of the relevant rights is examined, it is clear that the officer in charge is required to 'afford' to the suspect Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 47 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
only those rights which are completely within his or her power to fulfil.

In respect of the two rights where there is a duty to 'inform' the officer alone cannot ensure that they are ultimately fulfilled.

In this regard, although the entitlement to a reasonable opportunity to communicate (or to attempt to communicate) exists from the time of arrest, that opportunity can only be provided if and when the suspect desires to exercise the right. Therefore, it is necessary that the suspect should wish to communicate (with a friend or relative, and/or with a legal practitioner) before any reasonable opportunity can be afforded.

It is quite sensible and logical that s 138 does not explicitly require the officer in charge to 'afford' those rights at a time when he or she is not necessarily in a position to do so. It is also logical that the officer must first provide information on the existence of the rights which otherwise might not be known to the suspect. Without that knowledge the suspect would not be in a position to exercise the rights.

Although s 138 is silent as to the duty of an officer when a suspect expresses the wish to exercise one of the rights of communication, the Act clearly requires that there be a reasonable opportunity for the suspect to do so. Given that s 139(3) provides that an arrested suspect should ordinarily be detained in the company of an officer, any reasonable opportunity to communicate can only occur with the cooperation of that officer (eg by the provision of a telephone and telephone book).

In my view, it is also significant that s 138(4) refers to a 'right to communicate or to attempt to communicate' as distinct from a 'reasonable opportunity' to do so. This conversion in the nature of the rights conferred by s 137(3)(c) and s 138(2)(c) occurs in the context of the circumstances in which an officer may 'refuse' an arrested suspect that right. Clearly such a refusal can only be in response to a request by an arrested suspect to be able to exercise the right.

In light of these considerations it is my opinion that when the Act (and particularly s 137, s 138 and s 139) is construed as a whole, an arrested suspect's right to communicate arises as soon as he or she expresses the wish to exercise that right. In the absence of any reasonable suspicion under s 138(4) the officer then detaining the arrested suspect is obliged to afford the suspect that right.

sections.h:
Whether s 154 applies to evidence obtained in contravention of s 137 or s 138

In Malgil v The State of Western Australia [2008] WASC 290, Murray J at [20] held that s 154 and s 155 of the Act have 'nothing to do with the admission in evidence of confessional statements'. His Honour considered that no section of the Act allowed the court to make a decision under s 155 about 'the admissibility of confessional evidence obtained in breach of or outside the statutory processes'. His Honour adopted his conclusions in Malgil when making the rulings the subject of the present appeal. For the reasons which follow I respectfully disagree with his Honour's views.

When a police officer exercises a power conferred by the Act, s 154 takes effect if:

(a) a thing relevant to an offence is seized or obtained; and

(b) a requirement of this Act in relation to exercising the

power … including a requirement that arises before or after the exercise of the power … is contravened.

In those circumstances, any evidence derived from the thing or from the exercise of the power is not admissible against a person in any criminal proceedings unless the person does not object to admission, or the court decides otherwise under s 155.

The phrase 'thing relevant to an offence' has the extended definition in s 5. It is a fairly convoluted definition, but (relevant to the circumstances of the present case) provides that a thing is relevant to an offence if it is reasonably suspected that it is or may afford evidence relevant to proving the commission of an offence or who committed an offence.

Section 5(2) provides that such a thing 'may be material or non-material, animate (other than human) or inanimate'. The subsection also specifies examples of non-material things as 'the distance between two things or the visibility from a window'. Accordingly, s 5 enlarges the meaning of 'thing relevant to an offence' to a remarkable extent and far beyond the ordinary meaning of those words. The underlying purpose of this extended meaning was explained by the Attorney-General in the course of debate on the Bill as follows:

It is an all-encompassing definition that is designed to ensure that the powers and duties that are contained in the bill are sufficiently broad to Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 49

[2010] WASCA 199

The subsection also specifies examples of non-material things as 'the distance between two things or the visibility from a window'. Accordingly, s 5 enlarges the meaning of 'thing relevant to an offence' to a remarkable extent and far beyond the ordinary meaning of those words. The underlying purpose of this extended meaning was explained by the Attorney-General in the course of debate on the Bill as follows: It is an all-encompassing definition that is designed to ensure that the powers and duties that are contained in the bill are sufficiently broad to Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 49 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
encompass any thing in a practical sense that may be either evidence or the content of evidence. That is the most practical way in which this could be interpreted. For instance, the content of evidence might be the distance between two things. There might well be other things that are not necessarily material. In essence 'thing' means anything that is relevant to an offence. It is difficult to draw limits on the meaning of 'thing' but it is meant to convey the idea that it is very broad in its application (Hansard 1728).

In my view, it is self evident that a DVD disc containing an audiovisual interview by police officers of an accused will often be a material 'thing relevant to an offence'. It will be such a thing if it is reasonably suspected that the DVD may afford evidence relevant to proving the commission of an offence or who committed an offence.

Assuming that an interview on a DVD does afford such evidence, then in my opinion the interview itself (as distinct from the DVD) is a non-material 'thing relevant to an offence'. Given that the visibility from a window is a non-material thing, I consider that an interview must surely fall into the same category.

It follows that the evidence of such an interview is inadmissible under s 154 if it was obtained in the purported exercise of a power conferred by the Act and there was a contravention of any requirement in relation to the exercise of that power which arose before or after such exercise.

The relevant powers that are exercised when a police officer interviews an arrested suspect are those found in s 128 and s 139(2).

These are the powers to arrest a suspect and then to detain the arrested suspect for various purposes including interview. If an interview does occur in such circumstances, then the evidence of that interview is clearly derived from the exercise of both (and more particularly the latter) of those powers.

Sections 137 and 138 impose various requirements in relation to the exercise of the powers of arrest and of detention of suspects after arrest.

These requirements include the obligations on the officer in charge of the investigation to inform the suspect of his or her rights of communication, and to afford that suspect all other rights under the two sections. These requirements arise after the exercise of the first power, and either before or after the exercise of the second power.

Accordingly, I consider that on a literal construction of s 154 it applies to evidence of an interview obtained in contravention of any Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 50

[2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
requirement under s 137 or s 138. This literal construction also promotes one of the underlying objects of the legislation which is to balance the increased police powers under the Act with controls on those powers which protect the individual human rights of suspects. To my mind, Parliament could not have intended that s 155 be so restricted in its operation as to leave a lacuna in the Act in respect of the police powers under s 139. There is nothing in the language of s 154 and s 155, or in the extended definition of 'thing relevant to an offence' which would support such a narrow construction.

The operation of s 155 and its overlap with the common law Section 155 takes effect when any other section of the Act makes it applicable to evidence which is otherwise inadmissible. There are three such sections, and as a result, s 155 applies to:

• Evidence which is found in a 'protected forensic area' after the area

has been disestablished (s 48).

• Evidence of an unrecorded admission (s 118).

• Evidence obtained in contravention of a requirement of the Act

which relates to the exercise of a power or the issue of an authorisation (s 154).

There is a discretion to admit such evidence if it meets the test specified by s 155(2), namely that the court is satisfied that the 'desirability of admitting the evidence outweighs the undesirability' of doing so. This test is virtually the same as that which governs the exercise of a very similar discretion under the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth).

Section 138 of that Act provides:

138 Discretion to exclude improperly or illegally obtained evidence

(1) Evidence that was obtained:

(a) improperly or in contravention of an Australian

law; or

(b) in consequence of an impropriety or of a

contravention of an Australian law;

is not to be admitted unless the desirability of admitting the evidence outweighs the undesirability or admitting

evidence that has been obtained in the way in which the evidence was obtained.

[2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
This Commonwealth test is clearly a codification of the common law discretion to exclude evidence on public policy grounds, and it is relevant to note that s 90 of the same Act provides a separate discretion to exclude evidence of an admission on grounds of unfairness. The High Court considered the provisions of s 138 in Swaffield, and at [68] stated that the section expressed 'in the widest terms the policy discretion developed by the common law'.

Section 155 has a similar focus on public policy considerations. In subs (3), the matters to be taken into account under pars (b), (c), (d) and

(e) all bear a close resemblance to the factors relevant to the exercise of

the policy discretion at common law (as outlined by Deane J in Pollard at 203 - 204 - see [117] above).

In my view, each of those factors has the same relevance in the statutory setting as it does at common law. Accordingly, the weight to be given to factor (b) (the seriousness of the offence) will vary according to the 'heinousness of the crime' so that the more serious the offence, the more weight it should be given.

Similarly, the weight to be given to the seriousness of any contravention of the Act in obtaining the evidence (factor (c)) will be proportionate to such seriousness. Factor (d) effectively requires that much greater weight should be given to a contravention if it was intentional or reckless than if it arose from an honest and reasonable mistake of fact. There will be occasions when a contravention does not fit either of those categories (eg carelessness) and in such a case, the court will assess its seriousness and give the factor such weight as it thinks fit.

The probative value of the evidence (factor (e)) will depend not only on its content, but also (in the case of an admission) on its reliability and unequivocalness. The greater the probative value, the greater the weight that should be given to that factor. However, by reason of subs (4) the weight given to the probative value can never be enough on its own to justify admission of the evidence.

The remaining factors in s 155(3) that the court must take into account when exercising the statutory discretion are:

(a) any objection to the evidence being admitted by the person against

whom the evidence may be given.

…

(f) any other matter the court thinks fit.

Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 52

[2010] WASCA 199

Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 52 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
It is significant that these two factors are not limited in any way and that they extend beyond matters of public policy. Furthermore, factor (a) refers to 'any objection to the evidence being admitted' which necessarily includes any objection based on common law grounds. Accordingly, when exercising the statutory discretion under s 155 in relation to evidence of an admission, the court will sometimes be required to take into account matters which are ordinarily determined by application of the principles and processes of the common law. Such matters might include the question whether or not the admission was made voluntarily, whether it would be unfair to the accused to admit the evidence, and (to the extent that there was any impropriety not involving a contravention of the Act) whether the evidence should be excluded on public policy grounds.

This entwining of common law issues with the exercise of the statutory discretion will not always occur, because there will be many cases where it is not alleged that an admission was obtained in contravention of the Act. In such cases, the issues concerning voluntariness, fairness, or public policy considerations will be determined entirely on a common law basis and in accordance with the common law burdens of proof. However, if the grounds of objection do include a claim that the evidence is inadmissible by reason of a provision of the Act (and that claim is upheld), then the statutory discretion will overlap with and have primacy over the common law. The important question in these circumstances is what impact s 155 has on the common law processes and outcomes.

Quite obviously the Act cannot be construed in a way which subjugates the common law determination of voluntariness to the statutory decision under s 155(2). The question whether a confession is voluntary is an issue of fact which is not susceptible to a discretionary determination. In this regard, it is clear that Parliament intended that s 155 would codify the common law rather than change its fundamental principles. This was confirmed during the parliamentary debate on the Bill when the Leader of the House in the Legislative Council stated that the clause (which is now s 155) had the following purpose:

The reason the government ever sets out to codify common law is to make life a little more understandable for the users of the given legislation. This measure will in the main be used by police prosecutors and police in charge of a particular case. They are not lawyers; they are certainly not expert in common law. This is an attempt to set out the common law in a black-and-white codified form with respect to this part of this act. It is no more profound than that. It is designed to create a clear, black-and-white statement of what is and is not admissible under this act. (Hansard 7439) Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 53

[2010] WASCA 199

Such matters might include the question whether or not the admission was made voluntarily, whether it would be unfair to the accused to admit the evidence, and (to the extent that there was any impropriety not involving a contravention of the Act) whether the evidence should be excluded on public policy grounds. In such cases, the issues concerning voluntariness, fairness, or public policy considerations will be determined entirely on a common law basis and in accordance with the common law burdens of proof. However, if the grounds of objection do include a claim that the evidence is inadmissible by reason of a provision of the Act (and that claim is upheld), then the statutory discretion will overlap with and have primacy over the common law. The important question in these circumstances is what impact s 155 has on the common law processes and outcomes. Quite obviously the Act cannot be construed in a way which subjugates the common law determination of voluntariness to the statutory decision under s 155(2). The question whether a confession is voluntary is an issue of fact which is not susceptible to a discretionary determination. In this regard, it is clear that Parliament intended that s 155 would codify the common law rather than change its fundamental principles. This was confirmed during the parliamentary debate on the Bill when the Leader of the House in the Legislative Council stated that the clause (which is now s 155) had the following purpose: The reason the government ever sets out to codify common law is to make life a little more understandable for the users of the given legislation. This measure will in the main be used by police prosecutors and police in charge of a particular case. They are not lawyers; they are certainly not expert in common law. This is an attempt to set out the common law in a black-and-white codified form with respect to this part of this act. It is no more profound than that. It is designed to create a clear, black-and-white statement of what is and is not admissible under this act. (Hansard 7439) Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 53 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
However, the (partial) codification of the common law in s 155 does interfere with the common law discretions. The common law discretions all operate to exclude evidence, whereas the statutory discretion operates to admit evidence. Accordingly, in respect of evidence rendered inadmissible by s 48, s 118, or s 154, there is simply no place for the exercise of any of the common law discretions to exclude evidence on the grounds of unfairness, public policy, or because the prejudicial effect outweighs the probative value.

Nevertheless, in any case where there are alleged breaches of the individual rights conferred by s 137 and s 138, issues of unfairness and/or impropriety will often arise. Sometimes there will also be an issue as to the probative value of the evidence compared to its prejudicial effect. It is in this context that the court must take account of the consequent objections under s 155(3)(a) to the admission of the evidence. If the objections are found to be valid they will add weight to the 'undesirable' side of the balancing exercise under s 155(2).

In summary, it is my view that on a proper construction of the Act:

(a) Section 155 does not impact upon the common law determination

of the issue whether a confession or admission was made voluntarily.

(b) Nevertheless, when making a decision under s 155(2) in respect of

a confession or admission, the court must take into account any objection that the same was involuntary. This will require a concurrent determination of the issue of voluntariness. In the event of a finding that the confession was involuntary, the undesirability of admitting the evidence would necessarily outweigh the desirability of doing so.

(c) In respect of any evidence which is found to be inadmissible under

s 48, s 118, or s 154, there is no scope for the common law discretions to operate. Section 155 governs all discretionary considerations affecting the admissibility of evidence which is otherwise inadmissible under the Act.

(d) The burden of persuading the court to exercise the discretion under

s 155(2) rests on the prosecution. To the extent that the decision turns on disputed issues of fact, the prosecution must prove those facts on the balance of probabilities.

Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 54

[2010] WASCA 199

It is in this context that the court must take account of the consequent objections under s 155(3)(a) to the admission of the evidence. If the objections are found to be valid they will add weight to the 'undesirable' side of the balancing exercise under s 155(2). In summary, it is my view that on a proper construction of the Act: (a) Section 155 does not impact upon the common law determination of the issue whether a confession or admission was made voluntarily. (b) Nevertheless, when making a decision under s 155(2) in respect of a confession or admission, the court must take into account any objection that the same was involuntary. This will require a concurrent determination of the issue of voluntariness. In the event of a finding that the confession was involuntary, the undesirability of admitting the evidence would necessarily outweigh the desirability of doing so. (c) In respect of any evidence which is found to be inadmissible under s 48, s 118, or s 154, there is no scope for the common law discretions to operate. Section 155 governs all discretionary considerations affecting the admissibility of evidence which is otherwise inadmissible under the Act. (d) The burden of persuading the court to exercise the discretion under s 155(2) rests on the prosecution. To the extent that the decision turns on disputed issues of fact, the prosecution must prove those facts on the balance of probabilities. sections.h:
Ground 1: Whether the trial judge erred in admitting evidence of the unrecorded admission

As the detectives were executing a search warrant at the time of the appellant's arrest, s 45(2) of the Act required that there be an audiovisual recording of that execution if 'reasonably practicable'. The issue whether it was reasonably practicable to record the execution of the search warrant (at the time of entry) essentially turns on the same considerations relevant to the question whether it was 'not practicable' to record the admission when it was made.

Detective Sergeant Leonhardt's evidence during the voir dire as to the circumstances surrounding the appellant's unrecorded admission was substantially unchallenged. The trial judge was entitled to accept that evidence which established the following facts relevant to the absence of a recording.

After the appellant's whereabouts became known to Detective Sergeant Leonhardt he had approximately one hour to make arrangements for the appellant's arrest. He organised a team of four detectives (including himself) to enter the house, and gave instructions that they were to 'locate Danny Wright, secure Danny Wright, secure the scene, and take him into custody'. The appellant was not to be questioned at the house, but was to be taken to the office of the Major Crime Squad for a formal recorded interview.

At the time of making these plans the Detective Sergeant had no way of knowing what his team was 'going to be confronted with' when they entered the house. He gave no thought to video recording the entry into the house and the arrest of the appellant. The reason why this did not occur to him was that he had never done so in similar circumstances before. It was also his view (in hindsight) that it was 'just not practicable to be running in there with video equipment when you enter the premises'.

There was no reason for Detective Sergeant Leonhardt to expect that the appellant would be at any particular location within the house, or that he might volunteer an admission prior to being interviewed. The admission occurred unexpectedly and in the first moments after the detectives entered the lounge room of the house. The admission was neither sought nor prompted by any detective but was made spontaneously by the appellant to his cousin in reaction to their sudden arrival. There is no way of knowing whether the admission might have been captured on video if one of the detectives had been carrying audiovisual equipment.

Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 55

[2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
Although no questions were put to Detective Sergeant Leonhardt, and there was no evidence concerning the audiovisual equipment available to him at the material time, it is probably reasonable to assume that such equipment could have been obtained. It is also reasonable to assume (given the seriousness of the offence being investigated) that an additional officer could have been made available to operate the equipment.

Nevertheless, it would have been difficult to have the equipment running at the time of entry into the house. Of necessity the detectives were moving swiftly, and they did not know what they would be confronted with once they were inside. They were entering unfamiliar premises, and it would have been difficult for a detective to operate a video camera while at the same time watching where he or she was going.

(To do so would have raised occupational health concerns). Almost certainly, the operator would have had to be the last one to enter, and would have been lagging behind.

In all of these circumstances it was open to the trial judge to find that it was 'not practicable' to record the admission at the time it was made, and in my view his Honour was correct with that finding. It follows that there was a reasonable excuse for the absence of an audiovisual recording of the admission. It also follows that ground 1 of the appeal must fail.

Ground 2: Whether the trial judge erred in finding that the video record of interview was voluntary

The appellant does not challenge the finding that the unrecorded admission was voluntary. However, he contends that the trial judge should have found that the video record of interview was involuntary on two grounds. Firstly, he did not want the interview to proceed because he was intoxicated by cannabis, and secondly, the interview proceeded despite the appellant wanting a lawyer.

With regard to the first ground, there was no direct evidence of the physical effects of the appellant's prior consumption of cannabis at the time of being interviewed. The results of analysis of his blood sample combined with the evidence of Professor Joyce established merely a possibility of some impairment of thinking functions equivalent to that which would arise from a mild degree of alcohol intoxication.

This being so, the State bore the burden of establishing on the balance of probabilities that the appellant's consumption of cannabis had not affected his capacity to exercise appropriate judgments in the course Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 56

[2010] WASCA 199

Ground 2: Whether the trial judge erred in finding that the video record of interview was voluntary The appellant does not challenge the finding that the unrecorded admission was voluntary. However, he contends that the trial judge should have found that the video record of interview was involuntary on two grounds. Firstly, he did not want the interview to proceed because he was intoxicated by cannabis, and secondly, the interview proceeded despite the appellant wanting a lawyer. With regard to the first ground, there was no direct evidence of the physical effects of the appellant's prior consumption of cannabis at the time of being interviewed. The results of analysis of his blood sample combined with the evidence of Professor Joyce established merely a possibility of some impairment of thinking functions equivalent to that which would arise from a mild degree of alcohol intoxication. This being so, the State bore the burden of establishing on the balance of probabilities that the appellant's consumption of cannabis had not affected his capacity to exercise appropriate judgments in the course Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 56 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
of the interview process. The only evidence before the court that went to that issue was the video record of interview itself.

The video showed that the appellant was properly cautioned at the commencement of the interview and that he was reminded of that caution a number of times. The appellant made it clear that he understood the caution and that he realised that he did not have to speak to the detectives.

Nevertheless, he chose to do so.

It was apparent from the demeanour of the appellant on the video that he understood and had no difficulty in answering the questions that were put to him. The appellant was interrogated in a proper fashion, and he was not subjected to any persistent importunity, or sustained or undue insistence or pressure.

The appellant made three separate requests for a lawyer in the course of the interview, and he was offered the opportunity of communicating with a legal practitioner after each of those requests. The first time he did not take up the opportunity and was happy to continue with the interview.

The second time his attempt to contact the Legal Aid Commission was unsuccessful. The third time he was successful in contacting a lawyer, and as a consequence decided not to answer any further questions. The video did not reveal any adverse impact on the appellant as a result of the failure to contact a lawyer following the earlier requests. In each of those two instances he also chose to continue answering questions.

In my view, there was no evidence to suggest that in the course of the interview the appellant's will was in any way overborne or that he was incapable of deciding whether or not to answer the questions that were put to him. There is no basis for the contention that the interview was involuntary, and it follows that that aspect of ground 2 must fail.

Grounds 2 and 3: Whether the trial judge erred in admitting evidence of the video record of interview

The evidence on the voir dire established that the appellant was arrested at approximately 12.22 pm, and that he was first informed of his right to communicate with a lawyer when the interview commenced at 2.07 pm. Accordingly, there was a lapse of approximately 1¾ hours from the time when the appellant became an 'arrested suspect' until Detective Sergeant Leonhardt complied with the obligation under s 138(3)(a).

Detective Sergeant Leonhardt was not asked, and there was no explanation from him as to the reasons for this delay. In my view, it is Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 57

[2010] WASCA 199
sections.b: of the interview process. The only evidence before the court that went to that issue was the video record of interview itself. The video showed that the appellant was properly cautioned at the commencement of the interview and that he was reminded of that caution a number of times. The appellant made it clear that he understood the caution and that he realised that he did not have to speak to the detectives. Nevertheless, he chose to do so. It was apparent from the demeanour of the appellant on the video that he understood and had no difficulty in answering the questions that were put to him. The appellant was interrogated in a proper fashion, and he was not subjected to any persistent importunity, or sustained or undue insistence or pressure. The appellant made three separate requests for a lawyer in the course of the interview, and he was offered the opportunity of communicating with a legal practitioner after each of those requests. The first time he did not take up the opportunity and was happy to continue with the interview. The second time his attempt to contact the Legal Aid Commission was unsuccessful. The third time he was successful in contacting a lawyer, and as a consequence decided not to answer any further questions. The video did not reveal any adverse impact on the appellant as a result of the failure to contact a lawyer following the earlier requests. In each of those two instances he also chose to continue answering questions. In my view, there was no evidence to suggest that in the course of the interview the appellant's will was in any way overborne or that he was incapable of deciding whether or not to answer the questions that were put to him. There is no basis for the contention that the interview was involuntary, and it follows that that aspect of ground 2 must fail. Grounds 2 and 3: Whether the trial judge erred in admitting evidence of the video record of interview The evidence on the voir dire established that the appellant was arrested at approximately 12.22 pm, and that he was first informed of his right to communicate with a lawyer when the interview commenced at 2.07 pm. Accordingly, there was a lapse of approximately 1¾ hours from the time when the appellant became an 'arrested suspect' until Detective Sergeant Leonhardt complied with the obligation under s 138(3)(a). Detective Sergeant Leonhardt was not asked, and there was no explanation from him as to the reasons for this delay. In my view, it is Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 57 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
self-evident that there was a breach of the obligation to inform the appellant 'as soon as practicable' of his right to a reasonable opportunity to communicate or to attempt to communicate with a legal practitioner. It follows that there was a contravention of the requirement of s 138(3)(a).

That requirement related to the exercise of the power under s 139(2) to detain the appellant after arrest for the purpose of interview, and the evidence of the interview was derived from the exercise of that power.

Accordingly, the evidence was inadmissible under s 154(2).

This being so, there was no scope for the common law discretions to operate, and the only issue that the trial judge had to determine was whether the evidence of the interview should be admitted under s 155(2).

The factors required to be taken into account in making that decision were as listed in subs (3). Dealing with each of those factors in turn I make the following comments.

With regard to factor (a), it was necessary to take into account the objection that it would be unfair to the accused to admit the interview. In my view, that objection had considerable weight, not because the admissions were in any way unreliable, but because they might never have been made at all if the appellant had been informed as soon as practicable after his arrest of his right to a reasonable opportunity to communicate with a legal practitioner.

Factor (b) deserved considerable weight because the offences in respect of which the evidence was relevant were extremely serious. As to factors (c) and (d) there was no evidence to show that the contravention of the Act in obtaining the evidence was intentional or reckless, but it was of a fairly serious nature and deserving of significant weight.

In my view, the evidence in the video record of interview had limited probative value. The admission that the appellant had left the station at the same time as Ms Gojiao could be readily established by other evidence. The only significant probative value of the admissions made during the interview came from the denial of the unrecorded statement, which provided the basis for an 'Edwards' lie' submission at trial.

With regard to factor (f), I do not consider that there was any other matter that should have been taken into account in making the decision under subs (2).

The trial judge did not apply the provisions of s 155, and it accordingly falls to this court to determine in what way the discretion under subs (2) should have been exercised. In my view, on balance, and Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 58

sections.h:
after taking into account all of the relevant factors, the desirability of admitting the evidence of the interview was outweighed by the undesirability of admitting that evidence.

It follows that the trial judge erred in law in admitting the evidence of the interview and that ground 3 should be upheld. It also follows (pursuant to s 30(3) of the Criminal Appeals Act 2004 (WA)) that this court must allow the appeal if it is of the opinion that the appellant's conviction should be set aside because of that error of law. If it is of that opinion, the court may nevertheless dismiss the appeal (pursuant to the proviso in s 30(4)) if it considers that no substantial miscarriage of justice has occurred.

The principles governing the application of the proviso In a number of decisions the High Court has considered the statutory equivalents in other jurisdictions of the proviso in s 30(4) of the Criminal Appeals Act 2004. The leading authority is Weiss v The Queen (2005) 224 CLR 300 where the High Court reached a unanimous decision in respect of the Victorian proviso (which is substantially the same as our own). Other decisions include Wilde v The Queen (1987) 164 CLR 365, and Gassy v The Queen (2008) 236 CLR 293. In cases such as the present (involving evidence that was wrongly admitted) Weiss establishes the following principles:

(1) When deciding whether to apply the proviso the appellate court

must not speculate on what the trial jury would have done if the evidence had not been admitted, or on what a hypothetical future jury would do. Rather it must decide for itself whether a substantial miscarriage of justice has actually occurred (Weiss [35] and [39]).

(2) The decision is an objective one which is not materially different

from other appellate tasks. It requires an independent assessment of the admissible evidence to determine whether that evidence proved beyond reasonable doubt that the accused was guilty of the offence of which he or she was convicted (Weiss [39] and [41]).

(3) The assessment of the evidence must be based on the record of

trial, regardless of the advantages, disadvantages and natural limitations in doing so (Weiss [39] and [41]).

(4) This task must be undertaken on the whole of the record of trial

including the jury's verdict of guilty. In cases where it is possible Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 59

[2010] WASCA 199
sections.b: after taking into account all of the relevant factors, the desirability of admitting the evidence of the interview was outweighed by the undesirability of admitting that evidence. The principles governing the application of the proviso In a number of decisions the High Court has considered the statutory equivalents in other jurisdictions of the proviso in s 30(4) of the Criminal Appeals Act 2004. The leading authority is Weiss v The Queen (2005) 224 CLR 300 where the High Court reached a unanimous decision in respect of the Victorian proviso (which is substantially the same as our own). Other decisions include Wilde v The Queen (1987) 164 CLR 365, and Gassy v The Queen (2008) 236 CLR 293. In cases such as the present (involving evidence that was wrongly admitted) Weiss establishes the following principles: (1) When deciding whether to apply the proviso the appellate court must not speculate on what the trial jury would have done if the evidence had not been admitted, or on what a hypothetical future jury would do. Rather it must decide for itself whether a substantial miscarriage of justice has actually occurred (Weiss [35] and [39]). (2) The decision is an objective one which is not materially different from other appellate tasks. It requires an independent assessment of the admissible evidence to determine whether that evidence proved beyond reasonable doubt that the accused was guilty of the offence of which he or she was convicted (Weiss [39] and [41]). (3) The assessment of the evidence must be based on the record of trial, regardless of the advantages, disadvantages and natural limitations in doing so (Weiss [39] and [41]). (4) This task must be undertaken on the whole of the record of trial including the jury's verdict of guilty. In cases where it is possible Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 59 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
to conclude that the inadmissible evidence had no significance in the determination of the verdict, the fact of the guilty verdict must form part of the appellate court's assessment of the whole record of trial (Weiss [43]).

(5) There will be cases where the natural limitations in assessing the

record of trial do not allow the appellate court to be satisfied as to the accused's guilt beyond reasonable doubt. In such a case the proviso does not apply, and there should be an order for a new trial (unless there are exceptional circumstances justifying a verdict of acquittal) (Weiss [41]).

(6) The phrase 'no substantial miscarriage of justice' cannot be

universally defined. However, the appellate court will not be in the position to make this determination if it is not satisfied that the admissible evidence proved beyond reasonable doubt the accused's guilt of the offence of which he or she was convicted (Weiss [44]).

(7) If the appellate court is satisfied that the admissible evidence

proved the accused's guilt beyond reasonable doubt, this does not foreclose the possibility of circumstances which justify allowing the appeal and ordering a new trial. Such circumstances would include 'a significant denial of procedural fairness', or errors amounting to 'such a serious breach of the presuppositions of the trial as to deny the application of the … proviso' (Weiss [45] - [46]).

With regard to (7) above, the majority of the court in Wilde (at 373) considered that it was necessary to allow an appeal (notwithstanding satisfaction as to guilt) if there were procedural errors of a kind 'so radical or fundamental' that their very nature excluded the application of the proviso. Such errors included any departure from the essential requirements of the law which went to 'the root of the proceedings'.

However, in Gassy, Gummow and Hayne JJ at [33] disapproved of 'a taxonomy of errors at trial which described some as "fundamental" and others as not'. Their Honours also observed that judicial statements about the application of the proviso cannot be used in substitution for the statutory test, namely whether the appellate court considers that no substantial miscarriage of justice has occurred. In this regard, it was held in Weiss at [42]:

It is not right to attempt to formulate other rules or tests in so far as they distract attention from the statutory test. It is not useful to attempt that Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 60

[2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
task because to do so would likely fail to take proper account of the very wide diversity of circumstances in which the proviso falls for consideration.

It follows that the circumstances in which there might be a substantial miscarriage of justice (notwithstanding the appellate court's satisfaction as to the guilt of the appellant) are not closed. As stated in Weiss at [45] and [46], a 'significant denial of procedural fairness' and a 'serious breach of the presuppositions of trial' are simply examples of such circumstances.

Whether the admissible evidence established the guilt of the appellant Accordingly, it is necessary to carry out an independent assessment of the admissible evidence at trial to determine whether or not it proved beyond reasonable doubt the appellant's guilt of each of the offences of which he was convicted. This evidence includes the appellant's evidence, and it is not suggested on appeal that he would have chosen not to testify if the video record of interview had been excluded. In that regard, the forensic evidence against the appellant was very compelling, and failing a plausible explanation consistent with innocence, the prospects of conviction were very high. Accordingly, there is nothing unfair in this court taking account of the appellant's evidence (other than that concerning his admissions during the video record of interview) in determining whether or not the proviso applies.

The route taken by the appellant

The appellant's evidence included his admission (ts 1319, 1344 - 1346) that he was the male person in the CCTV footage (exhibits 1 and 13) who was seen to be exiting Stirling Train Station at the same time as, and behind, Ms Gojiao. They both exited the station at 11.11 pm (ts 251). There was an issue as to whether or not the appellant then continued to follow Ms Gojiao. In this regard, it was the appellant's evidence that he waited at a bus stand outside the station for 'no more than two or three minutes' with the intention of catching a bus to Gwelup. He then decided that he would instead 'catch a cab' at Innaloo Shopping Centre. For this reason he walked along Cedric Street to Ellen Stirling Boulevard and continued along a bike path which led him 'down a little dark street' towards the shopping centre. He was not aware that he was following anyone while doing this (ts 1321 - 1322).

However, there was also evidence from Mr Tuan Tsan who at that time was walking the same route in reverse with the intention of catching Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 61

However, there was also evidence from Mr Tuan Tsan who at that time was walking the same route in reverse with the intention of catching Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 61 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
the 11.20 pm train from Stirling Station. (He in fact just missed the 11.20 pm train). While crossing Ellen Stirling Boulevard Mr Tsan saw 'a girl and a man' coming in the opposite direction. The girl was of Asian appearance and the man was walking five to ten metres behind her. They were both 'walking quite fast' (ts 269). After Mr Tsan passed them he looked back and saw the man and girl crossing Ellen Stirling Boulevard.

By then the other two were 'a little bit closer' to each other (ts 271) and 'she looked like she was walking towards Oswald Street' (ts 281).

The appellant's evidence that (after leaving the bus stand) he took that same route along Cedric Street, across Ellen Stirling Boulevard, and along the only footpath down Oswald Street, was a significant admission.

It was the route that Ms Gojiao habitually took to reach her home (ts 215, 218). It also placed him at or near the scene of the crime at about the time that it occurred (even though on his own evidence he continued on towards the Innaloo Shopping Centre without seeing Ms Gojiao).

When the attack occurred

In that regard, the evidence as a whole gives rise to the compelling inference that Ms Gojiao was attacked as she was walking to her home from the train station. A resident at 79 Oswald Street (Mrs M Spatara) heard a 'short and sharp' yell by a female voice out the front of her house sometime between 11.10 pm and 11.25 pm (ts 350). At about 11.15 pm Mrs Beverley Gascoigne at 80 Oswald Street heard a 'loud exclamation' by a light voice which she thought to be female, closely followed by a 'muffled man's voice' (ts 323). Mrs Gascoigne's husband also heard a short exclamation or brief shout by a female voice for a 'split second' at some time between 11.15 pm and 11.30 pm (ts 339, 341).

Ms Gascoigne's evidence

Mrs Gascoigne was the only witness (other than the appellant) who saw anything of relevance at the material time. When she heard the exclamation she got out of bed, opened her front door and looked out through the flyscreen. She could not see or hear anything. However, while making her way back to her bedroom she decided to look out of the window of her study. Her evidence as to what she then saw was as follows:

I looked out the window there, I don't know what I was expecting to see, and while I was there a man walked down the street, just on the footpath, and he just kept walking, and while I was still looking out the window I saw a shadow of a man but it was very dark and he just looked like he Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 62

…

When asked whether the tree was in front of the house at 75 Oswald Street, Mrs Gascoigne's evidence was: 'No. That would be 79 I think' (ts 331). Mrs Gascoigne's field of vision from the window was limited to the left and right by reason of the downward slope of her property from the street and some low brick walls. The man walking along the footpath was carrying what looked like a white cloth shopping bag (ts 326) and he came into Mrs Gascoigne's field of vision from the left. She saw him 'veer slightly at about the level of the tree and then continue walking' on the footpath and he passed out of her vision to the right (ts 331 - 332). The second man she saw was 'a shadow of a person which I presumed to be a man'. This happened 'probably only a few seconds' after the person with the white bag went out of view (ts 337). She could not see what he was carrying but saw the action of him 'pick his hand up and put it in the bin but what it was I don't know'.

sections.h:
The appellant's evidence

It was the appellant's evidence that while walking down Oswald Street towards the shopping centre he was doing so at a 'fairly fast pace' (ts 1324). He was walking along the only footpath near the kerb on the western side of the street, and carrying a white plastic bag. As he got to 'the bottom' of Oswald Street 'just before the roundabout' he stopped to look in his wallet to see how much money he had. As he had only $6 which was not enough for a taxi fare he decided to turn around and 'walk home' (ts 1325).

Before the appellant turned around he heard 'like a fight or a scream or something' (ts 1326). When he had walked half way back along Oswald Street (in the vicinity of number 75) he saw two people 'underneath a tree or something' and:

It either looked like they were having sex or one figure was laying down;

the other person was sort of like leaning over or kneeling or something.

(ts 1326).

The appellant did not pay much attention and kept on walking. His evidence-in-chief as to what then happened was as follows:

As you continued along Oswald Street what happened?---I heard a loud noise behind me back in the direction that I just walked. This time I turned around and on the opposite side of the road that the footpath was on I seen a couple of kids or I don't know if they were kids, teenagers or whatever, come out of a house and as I've seen them I've seen a person that was underneath the tree jump up, put something in a bin and then he sort of like looked back and run towards me and he's run towards me he's run past. I didn't really look at him or get a good description and that's about it.

That person that passed you did you see them do anything?---As what? As I just said he just put something - he just stood up from the body.

Yes?---Put something in the bin.

…

And you have seen a person come from where and put something in the bin?---From - I assumed it was the two people that I had just walked past.

You assumed that it's the two people that you had just walked past. All right. What did you do, if anything, then?---Stood there for a second sort of spinning out, going 'What's going on?' you know, like why is this dude just finishing - you know, whatever he was doing - I thought he was having sex. He's run past and I sort of thought it was a bit weird so I've actually gone back to the tree. …

sections.b: The appellant's evidence It was the appellant's evidence that while walking down Oswald Street towards the shopping centre he was doing so at a 'fairly fast pace' (ts 1324). He was walking along the only footpath near the kerb on the western side of the street, and carrying a white plastic bag. As he got to 'the bottom' of Oswald Street 'just before the roundabout' he stopped to look in his wallet to see how much money he had. As he had only $6 which was not enough for a taxi fare he decided to turn around and 'walk home' (ts 1325). Before the appellant turned around he heard 'like a fight or a scream or something' (ts 1326). When he had walked half way back along Oswald Street (in the vicinity of number 75) he saw two people 'underneath a tree or something' and: It either looked like they were having sex or one figure was laying down; the other person was sort of like leaning over or kneeling or something. (ts 1326). The appellant did not pay much attention and kept on walking. His evidence-in-chief as to what then happened was as follows: As you continued along Oswald Street what happened?---I heard a loud noise behind me back in the direction that I just walked. This time I turned around and on the opposite side of the road that the footpath was on I seen a couple of kids or I don't know if they were kids, teenagers or whatever, come out of a house and as I've seen them I've seen a person that was underneath the tree jump up, put something in a bin and then he sort of like looked back and run towards me and he's run towards me he's run past. I didn't really look at him or get a good description and that's about it. That person that passed you did you see them do anything?---As what? As I just said he just put something - he just stood up from the body. Yes?---Put something in the bin. … And you have seen a person come from where and put something in the bin?---From - I assumed it was the two people that I had just walked past. You assumed that it's the two people that you had just walked past. All right. What did you do, if anything, then?---Stood there for a second sort of spinning out, going 'What's going on?' you know, like why is this dude just finishing - you know, whatever he was doing - I thought he was having sex. He's run past and I sort of thought it was a bit weird so I've actually gone back to the tree. … Document Name: WASCA\CACR\2010WASCA0199.doc (JP) Page 64 [2010] WASCA 199

sections.h:
As you have approached the tree were you carrying anything at that stage?---Yeah, the whole time I was carrying a white bag.

As you approached the tree what did you see?---it wasn't until I got pretty close that I've seen someone laying on the ground and she was half naked.

What did you do then?---I didn't get too close but I like said something, you know, like, 'Are you all right?' or any - I can't remember exactly what I said. Got no response. I had a look in the bin.

Yes?---I've pushed stuff around, like, just quickly like got the bin - the bag in my right hand, I've got the bin open like that and I've just sorted - went like that to see if there was anything out of the ordinary, saw nothing out of the ordinary, looked back at the figure and after that I just walked off.

All right. Now, you've looked back at the figure. Did anything occur to you about the figure that you saw at that time?---Yeah, I assumed she was dead.

What made you assume that she was dead?---Well, she was not responding and she was laying there half naked.

At that particular point of time what went through your mind and what action, if any, did you take?---A whole heap of circumstances went through my mind. Just being out of gaol with a criminal record, I panicked and left. I didn't - you know, I didn't want to be involved in any way.

(ts 1327 - 1329)

During cross-examination, it was the appellant's evidence that when he walked past the couple under the tree he was five metres away but it was too dark for him to make out whether they were two males or a male and a female (ts 1354 - 1355). When he heard the second noise and looked back there could have been three or four people who had come out of the house on the opposite side of the road (ts 1356). The appellant was then cross-examined as to why he had stated in evidence-in-chief that the person under the tree had stood up 'from the body':

I just want you to accept that's what you said yesterday, 'He just stood up from the body.' That's what you said, wasn't it?---Splitting of hair, splitting of words.

Did you say that yesterday, Mr Wright?---If that's what I said then that's what I said.

Because you didn't know at that stage it was [a] body, did you?---Put two and two together and assumed it was.

sections.h:
At that stage could you?---When you saw that person stand up you thought it was a body, did you?---Well, it was just from where I'd seen two people previously so why wouldn't I assume that it was a body on the ground.

So when you saw that person stand up you thought he was standing up from a body, did you?---I now say 'body' but I thought he was standing up from a person.

All right. Because yesterday, as I say, you said, 'He just stood up from the body'?---Yes.

And then he went to the bin?---Yes.

Yes, and then ran towards you?---No, he looked back down at the body, bent down did something and then ran towards me, yes.

What did he do to the body?---I wouldn't have a clue.

What did he seem to do to the body?---Bent down. I don't know; picked something up, maybe. I'm not sure.

And this was whilst he was under the tree, was it?---Yes.

So you could see all that, could you?---Sort of.

Despite the fact that it was so dark?---That's why I just said 'sort of'.

I see. You saw enough to see him lean down and do something to the body?---Yes.

How far away were you at this stage?---15, 20 metres maybe. (ts 1357) The appellant confirmed that when he returned to the tree he did not touch the person on the ground or any of her clothes (ts 1361 - 1362). He went back to the tree out of curiosity and also looked in the bin because of his curiosity or stupidity (ts 1360, 1364).

The appellant opened the bin with his right hand (which was holding the white shopping bag) and used his left hand to 'have a look inside' (ts 1364). He touched 'just rubbish' and had no idea whether he touched any bottles. He did not grip anything and was not conscious of touching anything with blood on it. He did not notice any blood on his hand afterwards and assumed that he would have noticed blood if it had been there (ts 1365).
